title: TYPE_CHECKING for Avoiding Circular Imports

glossary.definition: An error that occurs when two or more modules attempt to import each other, creating an infinite loop.
Programs that analyze your code without running it to find potential errors, like incorrect data types.

overview.what:
In Python, using type hints often requires importing classes from other modules. If two modules import each other just for type hinting, it creates a 'circular import' error at runtime. `typing.TYPE_CHECKING` is a special constant that is `True` when the type checker (like mypy) is running, but `False` at runtime. By placing type-only imports inside an `if TYPE_CHECKING:` block, you hide them from Python at runtime, preventing the circular import.

The mental model is 'two parallel universes': one universe where the type checker runs (and sees the imports), and another where Python runs the code (and skips the imports).
overview.why: It solves the catch-22 of needing to import a module to annotate types, but not being able to import it because it would cause a circular import crash.

why_learning_this: You can type hint complex architectures without restructuring your entire codebase to avoid import cycles.
It reduces runtime overhead because type-only modules aren't loaded into memory.

common_mistakes.title: Using the imported name directly at runtime
common_mistakes.explanation: If an import is guarded by `if TYPE_CHECKING:`, it doesn't exist at runtime. Using it outside of string-based type hints (or `from __future__ import annotations`) will cause a `NameError`.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting string annotations
common_mistakes.explanation: Without `from __future__ import annotations`, you must use string literals for types imported under `TYPE_CHECKING`, like `def foo(user: 'User'):`.

recall_questions.q: What is the value of `typing.TYPE_CHECKING` when a Python script is executed?
How does `TYPE_CHECKING` prevent circular import errors?
recall_questions.answer: It hides type-only imports from the Python runtime interpreter by placing them in an `if TYPE_CHECKING:` block, while the static type checker still sees them.

understanding_checks.question: What does this code print when executed by Python?
understanding_checks.why: At runtime, TYPE_CHECKING is always False, so the code inside the else block executes.

understanding_checks.question: Where is the runtime bug in this code?
understanding_checks.why: The User class is defined inside the TYPE_CHECKING block, so it doesn't exist at runtime. You cannot instantiate it.

aha_moment.prediction: What is the value of TYPE_CHECKING at runtime?
aha_moment.common_guess: True, because type checking type-hints are built into Python.
aha_moment.why: It prints False. Python itself does not enforce types at runtime; the static type checker runs entirely separately.

practice_tasks.title: Fix the NameError
practice_tasks.prompt: This code raises a NameError because Database is imported under TYPE_CHECKING but used natively as a type hint. Fix the type hint so it works at runtime by making it a string.

challenge.title: Future annotations
challenge.prompt: Instead of string quotes, use `from __future__ import annotations` at the top of the file so `Database` can be written normally.
